strategies.name: Multi-use outer binop blocks equal-op distributive fold
strategies.target: Mutate an existing `or/and/xor` shift-combine test into `%sx = shl i32 %x, 3; %base = or i32 %sx, %m; %sy = shl i32 %y, 3; %r0 = or i32 %base, %sy; %r1 = xor/add/or i32 %base, %z; ret/use both`, so `%base` has more than one use while both shifts remain one-use.
strategies.rationale: The patch specifically guards the enclosing `m_c_BinOp`, and the earliest fold path (`BinOpc == I.getOpcode()` with bitwise ops or `add`+`shl`) does not require constant shift/mask. A shared intermediate such as `%base = or (shl %x, C), %mask` feeding two downstream `or` consumers was the reported growth pattern. This is the most direct regression target for the fix.
strategies.expected_issue: If the one-use guard is incomplete, InstCombine still rewrites the target consumer of the shared intermediate, leaving the original intermediate for the other use and adding a fresh unshifted binop plus shift/top binop. The CHECK should show no duplicated reassociated shift fold for that consumer when the intermediate has multiple users.

strategies.name: Multi-use outer binop blocks constant-mask transfer fold
strategies.target: Create/mutate a test like `%sx = lshr i32 %x, 4; %base = xor i32 %sx, 240; %sy = lshr i32 %y, 4; %r0 = and i32 %base, %sy; %r1 = add i32 %base, %z; ret/use both`. Also vary top/binop pair among `and` with `or/xor/add` and `shl` cases with a constant shift amount.
strategies.rationale: The second fold path shifts a constant mask with `GetInvShift` and applies `CanDistributeBinops`. It has different profitability and legality checks from the equal-op path. The added `m_OneUse` should dominate this path too, especially for cases where top binop is `and` and thus `CanDistributeBinops` accepts any constant mask.
strategies.expected_issue: InstCombine may still fire on a shared outer binop in the constant-mask path, causing new mask-shifted/reassociated instructions to be created while the old outer binop remains live for the extra use.

strategies.name: Multi-use outer binop blocks AShr-not fold
strategies.target: Mutate an arithmetic-shift test into `%sx = ashr i32 %x, 5; %notx = xor i32 %sx, -1; %sy = ashr i32 %y, 5; %r0 = and/or/xor i32 %notx, %sy; %r1 = add/xor i32 %notx, %z; ret/use both`.
strategies.rationale: The AShr path has separate semantics: it recognizes `BinOp2 == xor` with all-ones mask as a `not`, and only top-level bitwise ops are allowed. Because this path does not go through constant mask shifting or `CanDistributeBinops`, it needs an explicit regression test that the new outer one-use wrapper applies to it as well.
strategies.expected_issue: The arithmetic-shift/not special case may still transform one user of a shared `xor (ashr X,C), -1` into `ashr (binop Y, not X), C`, leaving the original `xor` live and increasing IR.

strategies.name: Operand-commutation coverage for multi-use outer binop
strategies.target: Generate four variants from the same base: top-level as `binop %base, %sy` and `binop %sy, %base`; intermediate as `or %sx, %mask` and `or %mask, %sx`; add an extra use of `%base` in every variant. Use FileCheck to ensure `%base` remains and no folded `shift (binop %x,%y)` replacement appears.
strategies.rationale: `MatchBinOp(0)` and `MatchBinOp(1)` try both top-level operand positions, and `m_c_BinOp` may match the shifted value on either side of the intermediate binop. The new `m_OneUse(m_c_BinOp(...))` must guard the actual intermediate regardless of operand order. Missing a commuted form would preserve the original bug for syntactically different but canonicalizable IR.
strategies.expected_issue: A matcher-order bug could allow the fold when the shared outer binop is on the opposite operand of the top-level binop, or when the shifted operand and mask are commuted inside the intermediate binop.

strategies.name: Vector and non-splat mask multi-use guard
strategies.target: Mutate scalar tests to `<2 x i32>`/`<4 x i8>` with a vector constant shift amount and non-splat mask, e.g. `%sx = lshr <2 x i32> %x, <i32 1, i32 3>; %base = or <2 x i32> %sx, <i32 2, i32 24>; %sy = lshr <2 x i32> %y, <i32 1, i32 3>; %r0 = xor/and <2 x i32> %base, %sy; %r1 = and <2 x i32> %base, %z; ret/use both`.
strategies.rationale: `CanDistributeBinops` explicitly mentions non-splat vectors, and shifts are lane-wise poison when an element shift amount is out of range. The patch is profitability-oriented, but vector constants exercise different `ConstantFoldBinaryOpOperands` behavior and m_ImmConstant matching. The outer one-use requirement should be uniformly enforced for scalar and vector forms.
strategies.expected_issue: The fold may still cause IR growth for vector/non-splat constants or poison/undef element masks if the outer binop is multi-use, especially because the mask-distribution predicate has vector-specific comments and constant-folding behavior.

reason_thou:
### 1. Fix Understanding

`foldBinOpShiftWithShift` recognizes patterns where two same-opcode shifts with the same shift amount feed a top-level binop, while one shifted value is first combined with a mask/constant through an intermediate binop:

- Logic-shift constant-mask transfer:
  `Binop1(Binop2(logic_shift X, C), Mask), logic_shift Y, C)`
  -> `logic_shift(Binop1(Binop2(X, inv_logic_shift(Mask, C)), Y), C)` under distributivity/mask constraints.
- Equal-binop distributive fold:
  `Binop(Binop(logic_shift X, Amt), Mask), logic_shift Y, Amt)`
  -> `Binop(logic_shift(Binop X, Y, Amt), Mask)` for bitwise ops or `add` with `shl`.
- AShr-not special case:
  `bitwise_op(xor(ashr X, Amt), -1), ashr Y, Amt)`
  -> `ashr(bitwise_op(not X, Y), Amt)`.

Before this patch, both shift instructions were required to have one use, but the enclosing intermediate binop, `Binop2(shift X, Mask)`, was not. If that intermediate was shared by other users, replacing only the current top-level user could not eliminate the intermediate. The fold therefore added new instructions while leaving the old shared subgraph alive, causing IR growth. The patch wraps the outer `m_c_BinOp(...)` in `m_OneUse(...)`, so the fold is attempted only when the intermediate binop can be eliminated together with the matched shifts.

### 2. Formal Semantic Modeling

Let `S(v) = shift(v, c)` with the same logical or arithmetic shift opcode and amount on both sides. Let `B2(a,m)` be the intermediate binop and `B1(a,b)` the top-level binop.

Before the fix, the matcher accepted:

`R = B1(B2(S(X), Mask), S(Y))`

when `S(X)` and `S(Y)` were one-use, even if `B2(S(X), Mask)` had other users.

After the fix, the matcher accepts the same expression only if:

`hasOneUse(B2(S(X), Mask)) == true`.

The value-level rewrite is intended to be semantically equivalent under the existing distributivity constraints. The changed contract is profitability/non-growth: if the matched root `I` is replaced, then the matched intermediate binop and both matched shifts should become dead, so the new IR does not duplicate a shared base computation.

Concrete scalar illustration for the equal-op `or`/`shl` path:

```
%sx   = shl i32 %x, 3
%base = or i32 %sx, 5
%sy   = shl i32 %y, 3
%r0   = or i32 %base, %sy
%r1   = xor i32 %base, %z
```

For `%x = 2`, `%y = 4`: `%sx = 16`, `%sy = 32`, `%base = 21`, `%r0 = 53`. The fold for `%r0` would produce something like `%t = or i32 %x, %y = 6`, `%u = shl i32 %t, 3 = 48`, `%r0.new = or i32 %u, 5 = 53`. Semantically `%r0` is unchanged, but because `%r1` still uses `%base`, the original `%sx`/`%base` remain live and `%t`/`%u` are added. The after-fix precondition prevents this non-shrinking rewrite.

### 3. Assumptions and Preconditions Identified

- The intermediate binop wrapping the shifted `X` must have exactly one use for the transform to be profitable.
- Both matched shifts must still be one-use; the patch does not alter that existing precondition.
- The same shift amount SSA value must be used by both shifts, and both shifts must have the same opcode.
- The intermediate must be a real instruction, not a `ConstantExpr`.
- Top-level and intermediate binops must be among `and`, `or`, `xor`, `add`.
- For logical shifts, either the equal-binop distributivity path must apply, or the shift/mask constants must satisfy `shift(inv_shift(Mask, Amt), Amt) == Mask` unless top-level `and` or intermediate `and` relaxes the mask constraint.
- For `ashr`, the only accepted intermediate is `xor` with all-ones mask, and the top-level op must be bitwise logical.
- The matcher must enforce the new one-use condition independently of operand order, because the top-level matcher tries both sides and the intermediate uses `m_c_BinOp`.
- Vector constants and non-splat masks must obey the same one-use profitability rule as scalars.

### 4. Potential Cases to Break Assumptions

- Shared intermediate binop in the equal-op path: `%base = or/xor/and/add (shift X,C), Mask` has two users, one of which matches the fold. This directly violates the new profitability precondition.
- Shared intermediate binop in the constant-mask transfer path: different top-level/intermediate binops and constant masks exercise code after `CanDistributeBinops`; the new guard must apply before all of those subpaths.
- Shared `ashr`/`not` intermediate: the special arithmetic-shift path bypasses logical-shift constant-mask reasoning and still needs the same guard.
- Commuted operand forms: the intermediate may appear as either top-level operand, and the shifted subexpression may be either operand of the commutative intermediate binop. A too-narrow test could miss a matcher-order hole.
- Vector and non-splat constants: vector constant folding and poison-lane behavior differ from scalars, but the no-growth condition is structural and should not depend on element shape.

### 5. Test Strategies

The proposed strategies focus on negative tests where `%base` has multiple uses and therefore the fold should not occur. They should be built by mutating existing InstCombine shift/binop tests and using FileCheck to ensure the shared intermediate remains rather than being replaced by a newly reassociated `shift (binop X,Y)` form.

  %base = xor i8 %sx, %z

  %r1 = add i8 %base, %z
  %ret = xor i8 %r0, %r1

}

; Constant-mask transfer path: top-level and with an lshr and an intermediate
; constant-mask binop. The multi-use %base must block the fold.

define i8 @multiuse_outer_binop_blocks_const_mask(i8 %x, i8 %y, i8 %z) {
; CHECK-LABEL: @multiuse_outer_binop_blocks_const_mask(

; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP:%.*]] = xor i8 [[Z:%.*]], [[R0]]
; CHECK-NEXT:    [[RET:%.*]] = xor i8 [[TMP]], [[BASE]]
; CHECK-NEXT:    ret i8 [[RET]]
;
  %sx = lshr i8 %x, 2
  %sy = lshr i8 %y, 2
  %base = or i8 %sx, 12
  %r0 = and i8 %base, %sy
  %r1 = xor i8 %base, %z
  %ret = xor i8 %r0, %r1
  ret i8 %ret
}

; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP:%.*]] = xor i8 [[SY]], [[R1]]

; CHECK-NEXT:    ret i8 [[RET]]

  %sx = ashr i8 %x, 2

  %base = xor i8 %sx, -1
  %r0 = xor i8 %base, %sy
  %r1 = add i8 %base, %z
  %ret = xor i8 %r0, %r1
  ret i8 %ret

Each function keeps the two shift instructions otherwise eligible for the fold but gives the enclosing intermediate binop (%base) an additional use. This is exactly the structural condition that was missing before the patch. The equal-op, constant-mask, AShr-not, commuted, and vector cases exercise the distinct matcher/control-flow paths that could otherwise bypass the new m_OneUse(m_c_BinOp(...)) requirement. Alive2 verification reported no semantic miscompile for the optimized results, and differential execution of scalar tests under llubi (and vector tests under lli because llubi crashes on vector return postprocessing) showed matching outputs.

Fix Weakness Analysis: No correctness bug was found in the patched implementation. The tests are valuable regression coverage because an incomplete fix would still produce folded forms such as a new `shift (binop X, Y)` while leaving `%base` live for its extra use, increasing IR. The observed optimized IR preserves the shared intermediate in the targeted fold positions, with only unrelated reassociation/canonicalization of surrounding xor/add expressions.
